input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A 1/10‑scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensors and control technology, comprising lower and upper level chassis and autonomy elements. The vehicle operates in the automotive domain, performing Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) tasks. Its resource‑constrained electronic control units (ECU) and safety‑critical nature make it an ideal candidate for digital twinning, enabling real‑time monitoring, computation offloading, and remote management.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin offers three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a user interface that displays real‑time vehicle state, projected trajectory, and object tracks; allows viewing, saving, and report generation; (2) Computation Offloading – offloads the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, desired‑speed calculation) from the vehicle’s ECU to the DT, returning speed commands to the vehicle; (3) Remote Management – enables operators to remotely enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and issue emergency braking commands via a low‑latency communication channel.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Digital Twin computation and data exchange are synchronized using a shared time reference that must be accurate within 100 ms, as mandated by the V2X safety and computation offloading requirements. All data packages exchanged between the Physical Twin Entity and the Digital Twin Entity via ROS2 are timestamped in this reference frame, guaranteeing that time‑stamped sensor samples, point‑cloud clustering results, and control commands remain coherent across devices. This 100 ms clock accuracy constraint defines the temporal granularity of the DT services; higher‑frequency updates (e.g., 1 ms for automotive safety loops) are out of scope due to hardware limitations.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system implements a single, vehicle‑centric Digital Twin instance. The abstract reference architecture (ISO 23247) is realized with three entities (User, Digital Twin, Physical Twin), each instantiated as a ROS2 namespace and deployed on distinct hardware components (e.g., ECU, INS, LiDAR workstations). Although the DT spans multiple physical nodes, it is treated as a single logical twin for the vehicle. No separate distributed twin instances or federated architecture are defined within the provided design.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The DT is orchestrated following ISO 23247 reference architecture, mapping functional entities (FEs) to individual ROS2 packages containing one or more ROS2 nodes. The User Entity hosts the User Interface FE subdivided into a 3D visualization FE and a control‑panel FE; the Digital Twin Entity provides data‑management, pre‑processing, and control logic; and the Physical Twin Entity implements low‑level automation such as point‑cloud clustering/tracking and device communication. Each FE is packaged as a ROS2 node, and cross‑entity interaction is realized through ROS2 topics/services, providing both message‑based publish/subscribe and request/response patterns to coordinate computation and state.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the physical twin – LiDAR point clouds, motor status, steering and servo signals, and vehicle state telemetry – is captured by the Physical Twin Entity and first processed by the Data Translation component. This component serializes each stream, aligns its message type to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE format, and performs required unit conversions. Pre‑processing units such as the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software transform raw LiDAR returns into clustered object tracks and aggregate vehicle state for the digital twin. Enriched, serialized messages are then published over a ROS 2 network to downstream services such as 3D Visualization and Reporting FE. Inter‑entity transport between physical and digital twins occurs over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP at real‑time rates (typically 10–30 Hz for high‑frequency telemetry), enabling live dashboards and monitoring. The system relies on the Data Translation component for data‑quality assurance, ensuring consistent message framing, type alignment, and unit consistency across all streams.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The Digital Twin Services use a ROS 2‑based architecture; internal component communication takes place over ROS 2 topics leveraging DDS for message exchange, while external communication between the digital and physical twin entities occurs over Wi‑Fi using UDP as the transport protocol. The Data Translation component serializes control and sensor messages, aligning them across both layers. Although the documents do not specify exact latency or bandwidth requirements, real‑time dashboards imply sub‑100 ms end‑to‑end delay, and the LiDAR point‑cloud rate typically requires 10–30 Mbps. UDP provides best‑effort delivery; the system lacks a described reliability or flow‑control mechanism, and security protocols are not addressed in the provided materials.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: This Digital Twin system implements the ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Framework for manufacturing standard. It adopts the reference architecture defined in Part 2 of the ISO 23247 standard, organizing the Twin into an entity‑based structure comprising the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. Each entity is further decomposed into Functional Entities responsible for tasks such as OME actuation, data transformation, and user interaction. In terms of communication and interoperability, the system supports OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) as a viable implementation for data exchange and device control, following the specifications of the OPC UA standard. The architecture aligns with interoperability standards required for manufacturing digital twins, ensuring compatibility with industrial equipment and data sources. No additional certification or compliance frameworks are specified in the provided documents.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_
block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

(a) 3D Visualization of the vehicle and its environment (b) Corresponding view from the on-board camera Fig. 3: Visualizations available from the virtual cockpit _C. Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. _A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
